text.text: Brenda B. commented on this

🚨 California Employers: Think Twice Before Terminating or Laying Off an Employee on FMLA or CFRA Leave 🚨

Too often, we see companies — especially out-of-state employers with California-based employees — make a costly mistake: terminating or laying off someone who is out on FMLA (Family and Medical Leave Act) or CFRA (California Family Rights Act) leave.

Here’s why this can be a serious legal misstep and major liability:

✅ Job Protection: Both FMLA and CFRA provide job-protected leave. That means when the employee returns, they are entitled to the same or a comparable position. Terminating them while on leave (or right after) raises a strong presumption of retaliation or interference.

✅ California Is Stricter: CFRA protections often go beyond federal FMLA. Even well-intentioned employers from other states can easily violate California law if they rely solely on their corporate HR team’s advice without understanding CFRA nuances.

✅ High Risk = High Cost: Wrongful termination, retaliation, and interference claims can lead to back pay, reinstatement, emotional distress damages, and even attorney’s fees.

If you’re a California employee who’s been terminated, laid off, or pressured to resign while on FMLA or CFRA leave — you should immediately reach out to an employment attorney experienced in California law to evaluate your rights and options.

And if you’re an employer operating in California:
👉 Make sure your policies, managers, and decisions are reviewed by California-based HR professionals or employment counsel — not just your out-of-state HR team.
California employment law is unique, and “we didn’t know” isn’t a defense.

#CaliforniaEmploymentLaw #FMLA #CFRA #HRCompliance #EmployeeRights #EmploymentLaw #CaliforniaEmployers #WorkplaceLaw

subDescription.accessibilityText: 4 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text: Brenda B. commented on this

💡 California Pay Laws: Know Your Rights 💡

As HR professionals, it’s our responsibility to ensure employees are paid fairly, on time, and in compliance with California labor laws. Unfortunately, not all employers get this right — and it’s important that every employee knows what the law requires.

Here’s what every California worker should know 👇

💰 Regular Paydays
	•	California employers must pay employees on their designated paydays, as stated in the company’s pay schedule (often semi-monthly or biweekly).
	•	If your paycheck is late, your employer may owe “waiting time penalties” — up to your daily wage for each day the paycheck is delayed, up to 30 days.

🔚 When Employment Ends
	•	If you’re terminated: You must be paid immediately — all wages, unused vacation, and owed commissions are due on your final day.
	•	If you resign with at least 72 hours’ notice: You should be paid on your last working day.
	•	If you resign without notice: Final pay is due within 72 hours of resignation.

Employers have both a financial and communication responsibility to ensure every employee understands their pay schedule and receives what they’re owed — without delay. Transparency and compliance aren’t just legal requirements; they’re trust builders.

#CaliforniaLaborLaw #HRCompliance #EmployeeRights #HumanResources #Payroll #EmploymentLaw

text.text: Brenda B. replied to Audrey B.’s comment on this

🤰💼California Pregnancy Disability Leave (PDL): compliance is NOT optional. Brand new employees are eligible

A common compliance mistake I still see: assuming an employee isn’t eligible for Pregnancy Disability Leave because they’re “too new.”

In California, Pregnancy Disability Leave (PDL) applies from day one.

Even if an employee has only been with the company one month, employers may still be required to:
	•	Provide up to 4 months of job-protected PDL if medically necessary
	•	Engage in the interactive process
	•	Provide reasonable accommodations
	•	Maintain health benefits during PDL
	•	Protect against discrimination or retaliation

PDL is separate from FMLA and CFRA and does not have hours-worked or length-of-service requirements.

HR owns this compliance.
That means training managers, correcting assumptions, and responding quickly when pregnancy-related limitations are disclosed.

Getting PDL wrong isn’t just a policy issue — it’s a legal risk.

#CaliforniaHR #PregnancyDisabilityLeave #PDL #HRCompliance #EmploymentLaw

description.text: Senior Director of Human Resources | Strategic HR Leadership
description.accessibilityText: Senior Director of Human Resources | Strategic HR Leadership

subDescription.accessibilityText: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text: Brenda B. commented on this

🚨 When Senior Leaders Start Leaving, It’s Already a Crisis — Even If No One Wants to Say It Out Loud

When senior managers and directors begin to disengage or quietly exit, the impact isn’t isolated — it ripples through the entire company.

This is the butterfly effect of leadership attrition:

✨ Teams lose clarity and direction.
✨ High performers wonder if they should start looking elsewhere.
✨ Engagement drops as uncertainty rises.
✨ Culture shifts from proactive to protective.
✨ The best talent becomes vulnerable to poaching.

This is why organizations must treat leadership turnover and disengagement as a crisis moment, not a routine HR task to “backfill.”

⸻

So what can HR do to stabilize engagement and combat turnover?

1️⃣ Immediately diagnose the root cause — not just the symptoms 

2️⃣ Close the communication gap.
Silence breeds fear. HR should partner with executives to deliver transparent, steady communication that reduces speculation and reinforces direction.

3️⃣ Re-anchor the teams left behind.
Employees need clarity fast.
HR can help stabilize workflows and reset expectations so the team doesn’t drift.

4️⃣ Re-engage remaining leaders.
HR must give them support, training, and tools — not just more pressure.

5️⃣ Protect culture intentionally.
HR should reinforce values through behavior modeling, recognition, and consistent leadership expectations.

6️⃣ Rebuild trust through visibility.
When HR is visible, proactive, and honest, trust grows — and turnover slows

description.text: Business Development, Strategic Initiatives,Leadership, Employee/Provider Engagement
description.accessibilityText: Business Development, Strategic Initiatives,Leadership, Employee/Provider Engagement

subDescription.accessibilityText: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text: Brenda B. commented on this

Your manager can impact your mental health more than your doctor or therapist.

Let that sink in.

We don’t talk about it enough, but the majority of people don’t leave companies—they leave the people who were supposed to lead them. I’ve seen it more times than I can count. A talented, hardworking person shows up ready to learn, to win, to grow—and their light slowly burns out, not because they weren’t capable, but because they were managed instead of led.

There’s a difference.

A manager controls.
A leader empowers.

A manager says "Do what I say."
A leader says "Let’s figure this out together."

A manager reminds you who's in charge.
A leader reminds you why you matter.

I’ve sat across from people in interviews who didn’t lack skill—they lacked confidence because someone made them feel replaceable. I’ve seen great employees second-guess themselves because someone in a higher position chose ego over development. I’ve watched top performers silently walk out of companies because psychological safety didn’t exist.

This isn’t about being soft. It’s about being human.

Leadership is not a title. Leadership is stewardship. You are responsible for the energy of the team you walk into every day. You are responsible for protecting the standard, not your own comfort. You are responsible for developing people, not draining them.

If you’re in a leadership position, read this twice:

People don’t need perfect leaders—they need present ones. Leaders who listen. Who teach. Who coach. Who hold the line with fairness and clarity. Leaders who can say, "I don’t have the answer yet—but I’ll work with you to find it."

Because here’s the truth:
A good leader can change someone’s career.
A great leader can change someone’s life.
And a toxic leader? They can damage both.

If companies truly want retention, innovation, and growth—it starts with who they choose to lead.

Promote mentors, not managers. Promote builders, not bullies. Promote people who make others better—because that is the real ROI.

Strong cultures don’t happen by accident. They are built by leaders who understand the assignment:

Protect the people. Grow the people. Empower the people.

Because when you take care of the people, the people take care of the business.

text.text: Brenda B. replied to Gretchen Benes’ comment on this

Stepping into a HR leadership role in a fast-growing organization has been both energizing and intense.
At JumpstartMD, we’re operating at the intersection of healthcare, metabolic wellness, and growth. That means scaling teams, tightening compliance, strengthening culture, and building infrastructure — all at the same time.

HR isn’t just about policies.

 It’s about:
• Protecting the organization
 • Supporting clinicians and member-facing teams
 • Navigating complex compliance landscapes
 • Driving operational accountability
 • Building scalable systems in a people-first environment


Growth-stage healthcare companies don’t need reactive HR.
 They need strategic HR.
Excited for what we’re building. 🚀

#HealthcareLeadership #HRStrategy #PeopleOperations #WomenInLeadership #MetabolicHealth #WellnessIndustry #ExecutiveLeadership #ScalingOrganizations #HumanResources #GrowthMode #HRinHeels

subDescription.accessibilityText: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text: Brenda B. replied to Shannon Mead’s comment on this

Why Every Multi-State Employer Needs a California-Based HR Leader 🌴⚖️

Many companies operating in California still rely on an out-of-state HR team. Sure, it seems cost-effective… but in CA, that decision can get expensive fast. 💸

California has some of the strictest and fastest-changing employment laws in the country. From wage-and-hour rules to leave laws, meal breaks, and local ordinances—compliance here is its own specialty.

Without California-based HR expertise, companies risk:
• ❗ Costly penalties + lawsuits from accidental non-compliance
• ⚠️ Hiring, discipline, and termination mistakes due to unique CA rules
• 🤝 Employee relations issues when HR doesn’t understand the local culture
• ⏳ Operational headaches when new laws hit and no one is prepared

Investing in a California-based HR Director or team isn’t just about compliance—it’s smart business. Local HR leadership provides real-time support, stays ahead of constant policy changes, and builds trust with your CA workforce.

In a state where the rules are different, having HR on the ground isn’t optional—it’s essential. 🌟

#CaliforniaHR #HRCompliance #EmploymentLaw #CaliforniaBusiness #HRLeadership #PeopleOps #HRStrategy
